title: Merck — Oncology — briefing Q3-2025
html:
What Changed in Merck Oncology — Q3 2025

Generated: 2026-04-24 Quarter covered: July - September 2025 Data sources: Merck Q3 2025 earnings call (Oct 30, 2025), FDA actions, clinical data, ZoomRx study deliverables Reading time: ~12 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. Keytruda hit $8.1B in Q3 2025 — first quarter ever above $8B. Revenue grew 10% YoY (8% ex-FX), driven by strong demand in metastatic settings and robust earlier-stage cancer uptake, particularly in women's cancers (cervical, endometrial, breast).
  2. KEYTRUDA QLEX (subcutaneous) received FDA approval in September 2025 — the first and only subcutaneously administered immune checkpoint inhibitor. Approved across most solid tumor indications. Administration in ~1 minute vs. 30-minute IV infusion. Merck targets 30-40% patient transition by 2028.
  3. KN-689 (perioperative HNSCC) was approved by FDA on June 12, 2025 — first perioperative IO approval in locally advanced HNSCC. Median EFS 51.8 vs. 30.4 months. ZoomRx's pre-launch research (biomarker qual, ATU W1) now transitions to post-launch tracking.
  4. Merck announced $3B in cost cuts by end of 2027 — explicitly linked to preparing for the Keytruda LOE in 2028. The company is investing aggressively in pipeline assets across immunology, cardiometabolic, and ophthalmology to diversify.
  5. ESMO 2025 featured broad Merck oncology data — new research across tumor types and earlier stages of disease, reinforcing the perioperative expansion strategy.

2. Data Events That Matter

KEYTRUDA QLEX FDA Approval (September 2025)

  • What happened: FDA approved KEYTRUDA QLEX (pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph) for subcutaneous use in adults across most solid tumor indications. Launched late September 2025.
  • What it means: This is the centerpiece of Merck's LOE defense strategy. QLEX carries separate patents from IV pembrolizumab, potentially shielding revenue from biosimilar competition beyond 2028. However, the real test is adoption speed — initial J-code is temporary (J9999), with permanent code expected April 2026.
  • What Vinoth should say: "QLEX approval is the starting gun for the LOE defense. But the first 6 months will be slow — no permanent J-code, limited reimbursement certainty, physician inertia with formulation changes. The real signal comes Q2 2026 after the permanent J-code. Our SubQ IO study is perfectly timed to capture that inflection."

KN-689 FDA Approval and Post-Launch Dynamics

  • What happened: Pembrolizumab approved for PD-L1+ (CPS >=1) resectable LA Stage III-IVA HNSCC as neoadjuvant treatment, continued adjuvant with RT +/- cisplatin, then as monotherapy. First perioperative IO in HNSCC.
  • What it means: This opens a new patient population (~66,000 new HNSCC cases/year in US, with a significant LA resectable subset). But ZoomRx's pre-launch data shows the adoption infrastructure is not yet ready — PD-L1 reflexive testing is formalized in only 4/13 practices, and surgeons are the referral bottleneck.
  • What Vinoth should say: "We predicted this would be a slow-ramp launch, and our data supports it. The bottleneck isn't oncologist awareness — 70% know KN-689. It's the upstream chain: surgeon referral to MDT, PD-L1 testing availability at the MDT meeting, and the oncologist's ability to discuss NAT before the surgeon schedules surgery. We should pitch Merck a surgeon engagement study as the next logical research step."

Keytruda $8.1B Quarter

  • What happened: Q3 2025 Keytruda revenue of $8.1B, up 10% YoY. Growth driven by earlier-stage cancer uptake and continued metastatic demand.
  • What it means: Keytruda's growth is now coming from the TAM expansion thesis — perioperative NSCLC (KN-671) and HNSCC (KN-689) are adding patients who previously received surgery alone. Women's cancers (cervical, endometrial, TNBC) are also a meaningful growth vector.

3. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q3 2025

Company Brand Q3 2025 Revenue YoY Growth Key Signal
BMS Opdivo ~$2.4B +5% CheckMate-77T perioperative NSCLC; Opdualag (~$150M) growing
AstraZeneca Imfinzi ~$1.1B +15% PACIFIC long-term data; ADRIATIC (LS-SCLC) approved
AstraZeneca DATROWAY ~$20M N/A (new) Approved Mar 2025 for HR+/HER2- mBC; first partial quarter
Roche Tecentriq ~$900M -8% Declining as Keytruda dominates; Roche pivoting to tiragolumab combinations
Keytruda $8.1B +10% Context: ~40% of global IO market; first quarter above $8B

BMS — Opdivo ($2.4B, +5%) + Opdualag ($150M)

BMS Q3 2025 call (October 2025):

  1. CheckMate-77T perioperative NSCLC is BMS's counter-play to Keytruda's KN-671: BMS emphasized perioperative nivolumab in resectable NSCLC, positioning Opdivo as the alternative to perioperative Keytruda. BMS highlighted the chemotherapy + IO neoadjuvant approach as differentiated, particularly in PD-L1 <50% patients where the data is more competitive.

  2. Opdualag (nivolumab + relatlimab) remains a niche melanoma franchise: At ~$150M quarterly, Opdualag is growing but limited to melanoma. BMS is not aggressively expanding Opdualag into other tumor types — it remains a melanoma-first combination.

  3. BMS managing through Opdivo maturity: With ~40 approved indications, Opdivo's growth is now incremental. BMS acknowledged that perioperative IO and combinations are the remaining growth vectors. No significant new indications expected in the near term.

  4. Biosimilar timeline remains 2027-2028: BMS confirmed that nivolumab biosimilar entry is expected in the 2027-2028 timeframe. BMS is investing in lifecycle management (Opdualag, subcutaneous formulations) to defend the franchise.

Implication for Merck: Opdivo's perioperative NSCLC push is the most direct competitive threat to Keytruda in the near term. However, Keytruda's 10% growth on a base 3x larger than Opdivo demonstrates that market leadership is entrenched. The real question is whether BMS can carve a perioperative niche in specific biomarker subgroups where Opdivo's data may be differentiated.

Roche — Tecentriq (~$900M, -8%)

Roche Q3 2025 call:

  1. Tecentriq is in managed decline: Revenue fell 8% YoY as Keytruda and Opdivo continue to dominate across IO indications. Roche acknowledged competitive pressure in NSCLC and urothelial cancer, where Tecentriq was once a first-mover.

  2. Tiragolumab combinations are the pivot: Roche is investing in anti-TIGIT (tiragolumab) + Tecentriq combinations as a differentiation strategy. Phase 3 SKYSCRAPER trials are ongoing, but early results have been mixed. This is a pipeline bet, not a near-term commercial threat.

  3. Roche's broader IO strategy is shifting to ADCs and bispecifics: Roche signaled that the IO monotherapy/doublet era is maturing and future competition will be driven by ADC + IO combinations and bispecific T-cell engagers.

Implication for Merck: Tecentriq's decline removes a competitor from Keytruda's rearview mirror. The strategic takeaway is Roche's pivot to ADCs/bispecifics — this signals that future Keytruda competition won't come from another PD-1/PD-L1, but from next-generation modalities. Merck's own ADC strategy (via Daiichi Sankyo collaboration) must keep pace.

AstraZeneca — Imfinzi ($1.1B, +15%) + DATROWAY ($20M)

AZ Q3 2025 call:

  1. PACIFIC long-term data cements Imfinzi in Stage III NSCLC: AZ reported 5-year+ survival data from PACIFIC, demonstrating Imfinzi's durable benefit in unresectable Stage III NSCLC. This remains Imfinzi's anchor indication and a segment where Keytruda's perioperative approach is an alternative rather than a replacement.

  2. ADRIATIC approval in LS-SCLC adds a new indication: Imfinzi received approval for limited-stage small cell lung cancer based on ADRIATIC trial data. SCLC is an underserved market, and this positions Imfinzi uniquely — Keytruda's SCLC presence is primarily in extensive-stage.

  3. DATROWAY (datopotamab deruxtecan) launched with ~$20M in its first partial quarter: AZ's ADC for HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer entered the market in March 2025. While small now, this represents the ADC class that could eventually complement or compete with IO in certain settings.

  4. AZ noted Keytruda's perioperative expansion as a competitive consideration: Management acknowledged that Keytruda's perioperative NSCLC strategy (KN-671) could erode Imfinzi's Stage III franchise by treating patients earlier, reducing the pool reaching unresectable Stage III.

Implication for Merck: AZ's Imfinzi is the most dynamic IO competitor — growing 15% while Tecentriq declines and Opdivo grows only 5%. The ADRIATIC approval creates differentiation in LS-SCLC where Keytruda has no presence. DATROWAY is worth monitoring as the ADC + IO paradigm develops. The perioperative tension (treat early with Keytruda vs. treat Stage III with Imfinzi) is a key market dynamic to track.

Synthesis: What the Combined Q3 2025 Picture Tells Us

  1. Keytruda's ~40% global IO market share is widening, not narrowing. At $8.1B, Keytruda generates more revenue than Opdivo, Tecentriq, and Imfinzi combined (~$4.4B). The competitors are differentiating into niches rather than challenging Keytruda's breadth.

  2. The perioperative IO battleground is the most contested frontier. Keytruda (KN-671 NSCLC, KN-689 HNSCC) vs. Opdivo (CheckMate-77T NSCLC) vs. Imfinzi (AEGEAN NSCLC trials ongoing). This is where share shifts will happen over the next 2-3 years.

  3. Next-generation competition is modality-based, not IO-vs-IO. Roche's pivot to ADCs/bispecifics and AZ's DATROWAY launch signal that the future competitive threat to Keytruda isn't a better PD-1 — it's a different drug class (ADCs, bispecifics, cell therapy) that may reduce IO's role in certain tumor types.

  4. Biosimilar timelines are becoming relevant. Opdivo biosimilars in 2027-2028 and Keytruda LOE in 2028 will reshape the IO market economics. QLEX is Merck's defensive play; BMS is investing in Opdualag and next-gen combinations. The LOE era will reward the company with the strongest lifecycle management — which, today, is Merck (QLEX).

4. Commercial Pulse

Metric Keytruda Keytruda QLEX
Q3 2025 Revenue $8.1B ~$5M (partial quarter)
YoY Growth +10% N/A (new)
Annual Run Rate ~$32.4B Too early
Key Q3 Event First $8B+ quarter FDA approval, late Sept launch

Full-Year 2025 Outlook: Merck tracking toward ~$31-32B total Keytruda (including QLEX). Earlier-stage indications now contributing meaningfully to growth.

What Merck management said on the Q3 2025 call:

  1. "Keytruda remains the backbone of oncology" — management framed every indication expansion as extending the franchise, not just adding revenue. The tone was explicitly about building a Keytruda ecosystem that survives the 2028 LOE.
  2. KN-689 (perioperative HNSCC) launch was highlighted as proof of the perioperative expansion thesis working. Management positioned the approval as the second leg of the perioperative strategy after KN-671 in NSCLC.
  3. QLEX launched with temporary J9999 code. Management acknowledged: "Temporary code is creating expected billing uncertainty; permanent code coming April 2026." This was the first explicit admission that the J-code friction was suppressing QLEX uptake.
  4. On the 2028 LOE strategy, management outlined a 3-prong defense: perioperative indication expansion, SubQ (QLEX) conversion, and new indications pipeline. The framing was confident but structured — this is a prepared narrative, not improvised reassurance.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts pushed hard on QLEX uptake friction from the J-code — the concern is that billing uncertainty creates a longer adoption curve than management projects. Management's response ("expected billing uncertainty") was more defensive than proactive.
  2. The 2028 LOE question dominated Q&A. Analysts specifically asked for a "realistic post-LOE revenue floor." Management's 3-prong defense outline was detailed but did not quantify expected post-LOE revenue retention — a notable omission.
  3. The overall earnings call was Keytruda-centric. Everything else (vaccines, pipeline) was secondary in both management commentary and analyst attention. This concentration risk is itself a signal.

5. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Key Evidence This Quarter
1. QLEX adoption determines post-2028 floor INITIATED QLEX approved Sept 2025. Adoption clock starts. No revenue data yet.
2. KN-689 slow burn — surgeons are bottleneck STRENGTHENED KN-689 approved June 2025. ZoomRx ATU W1 shows 20-29% NAT baseline. Biomarker testing infrastructure immature.
3. Perioperative TAM expansion is right bet STRENGTHENED KN-689 approval + earlier-stage revenue growth confirm strategy.
4. MDT is the intervention point UNCHANGED No new post-launch MDT data yet.
5. Three-pronged LOE defense INITIATED QLEX + KN-689 + cost cuts all deployed in Q3. Strategy is in motion.

6. What to Watch Next Quarter (Q4 2025)

  1. QLEX initial revenue — the first real adoption datapoint. Even $20-30M would signal early traction.
  2. KN-689 post-launch listening sessions — are surgeons initiating KN-689 conversations? Is PD-L1 testing expanding?
  3. ATU W2 fielding — post-launch quantitative baseline. Expected August 2025 fielding with results in Q4.
  4. Permanent J-code timeline — confirmation of April 2026 effective date for QLEX.
  5. $3B cost-cut execution — any impact on Merck's oncology commercial infrastructure?

7. Conversation Starters for Merck

  1. "Your $8.1B quarter proves the perioperative TAM expansion is working. But our pre-launch data shows KN-689 adoption will be slower than you expect — the surgeon referral chain and PD-L1 testing infrastructure are the constraints. Let us show you exactly where the gaps are." — Positions ZoomRx's existing data as immediately actionable.

  2. "QLEX's first quarter will be a slow start — no permanent J-code, billing uncertainty, physician inertia. But our SubQ IO study will be the first to capture real conversation dynamics around formulation switching. We'll know before your sales data whether QLEX is changing prescriber behavior." — Preempts low Q4 QLEX numbers with forward-looking measurement value.

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
